votes.reason: The Python code correctly uses set equality to check if both strings contain the exact same unique characters, which perfectly matches the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the sets of characters in both strings are equal.
The Python code correctly checks if the two strings contain the exact same set of characters, matching the Coq specification.

The Coq spec defines char_in_string as membership check and same_chars_spec as result=true iff both strings have the same character sets. The Python code uses 'c in s' for membership and compares set(s0) == set(s1), which is equivalent: result is True exactly when both strings contain the same characters. The biconditional in Coq (result = true <-> forall c, char_in_string c s0 <-> char_in_string c s1) means result should be true iff the character sets are equal, which matches the Python implementation.

The Coq spec defines char_in_string as membership check and same_chars_spec as result=true iff both strings have the same character sets. The Python code uses 'c in s' for membership and compares set(s0) == set(s1), which is equivalent: result is True iff the character sets are equal. Both produce the same concrete boolean judgment for any input strings.

The Coq spec defines char_in_string as membership check and same_chars_spec as result=true iff both strings have the same character sets. The Python code uses 'c in s' for membership and compares set(s0) == set(s1), which is equivalent: result is True iff the character sets are equal. Both produce the same concrete boolean judgment for any input strings.

The Coq spec says result is true iff both strings have exactly the same set of characters. The Python code checks set(s0) == set(s1) and compares it to result, which is equivalent. The helper char_in_string matches character membership for single-character inputs.

Both define membership as character occurrence and same_chars_spec as equality of character sets (ignoring multiplicity); Python’s set(s) equality matches the Coq forall equivalence for ascii chars, and result is compared accordingly.
same_chars_spec checks that result matches equality of character sets, which aligns with the Coq iff over membership; char_in_string matches presence for single characters
